question: Will Audi Revolut have the highest constructor score at the 2026 F1 Japanese Grand Prix?
description:
This market will resolve to the constructor team that collects the most points at the 2026 F1 Japanese Grand Prix, currently scheduled for Mar 29, 2026.

In the case of a tie between two teams for most points, this market will resolve in favor of the team whose listed name comes first alphabetically.

As soon as the results for this event are known, this market will resolve.

If the 2026 F1 Japanese Grand Prix is canceled or rescheduled to a date after Apr 5, 2026, this market will resolve to “Other.”

The resolution source for this market will be official information from Formula 1 (F1).

events.title: Japanese Grand Prix: Which Constructor Scores 1st?
events.description:
This market will resolve to the constructor team that collects the most points at the 2026 F1 Japanese Grand Prix, currently scheduled for Mar 29, 2026.

eventMetadata.context_description: Mercedes' commanding pace in FP1 and FP2 at Suzuka has surged their implied probability to 75.5% for the first constructor points at the Japanese Grand Prix, with George Russell topping FP2 timesheets ahead of McLaren's Lando Norris by just 0.097 seconds and Charles Leclerc in third for Ferrari. Lewis Hamilton's P4 further bolsters their edge on the demanding Suzuka layout, where tire management and setup precision shine. Red Bull's Max Verstappen endured traffic-disrupted laps, dropping to P10 and tempering their 23% odds despite historical dominance here. McLaren (25%) and Ferrari (30.5%) lurk close after strong long runs, but no major incidents or weather shifts alter the trader consensus favoring Mercedes' current momentum amid dry forecasts and sprint qualifying looming.

question: Will Pierre Gasly get pole position at the 2026 F1 Japanese Grand Prix?
description:
This is a polymarket on the driver who achieves pole position at the 2026 F1 Japanese Grand Prix, scheduled for Mar 28, 2026.

If the 2026 F1 Japanese Grand Prix is canceled or rescheduled to a date after Apr 4, 2026, this market will resolve to “Other.”

This market will resolve in favor of the driver who is officially recognized by Formula 1 as having set the fastest time during the qualifying session for the 2026 F1 Japanese Grand Prix. The market will be settled based on the FIA's official qualifying results, regardless of any subsequent penalties, disqualifications, or changes to the starting grid.

For example, if a driver sets the fastest qualifying time but later receives a grid penalty or is moved down the starting order, the market will still resolve to “Yes” for that driver.

The resolution source will be the official Formula 1 website and a consensus of credible sports news reporting.

events.title: Japanese Grand Prix: Driver Pole Position
events.description:
This is a polymarket on the driver who achieves pole position at the 2026 F1 Japanese Grand Prix, scheduled for Mar 28, 2026.

If the 2026 F1 Japanese Grand Prix is canceled or rescheduled to a date after Apr 4, 2026, this market will resolve to “Other.”

This market will resolve in favor of the driver who is officially recognized by Formula 1 as having set the fastest time during the qualifying session for the 2026 F1 Japanese Grand Prix. The market will be settled based on the FIA's official qualifying results, regardless of any subsequent penalties, disqualifications, or changes to the starting grid.

For example, if a driver sets the fastest qualifying time but later receives a grid penalty or is moved down the starting order, the market will still resolve to “Yes” for that driver.

The resolution source will be the official Formula 1 website and a consensus of credible sports news reporting.

eventMetadata.context_description: George Russell leads trader consensus for Japanese Grand Prix pole at 54.5% implied probability, driven by Mercedes' superior pre-season testing pace and the W16's high-speed stability suiting Suzuka's flowing sectors like 130R and Spoon. Teammate Andrea Kimi Antonelli, the 18-year-old rookie standout from recent FP1/FP2 sessions, follows at 21.5% after posting competitive laps just behind Russell. Charles Leclerc's 14.5% reflects Ferrari's consistent qualifying edge but recent setup struggles on high-downforce tracks. Lewis Hamilton's 8.4% factors his Suzuka experience amid Ferrari adaptation. Markets price in Mercedes rest advantage post-Bahrain and Red Bull's ongoing balance issues, though qualifying volatility and weather could shift dynamics.

eventMetadata.context_description: George Russell leads trader consensus for Japanese Grand Prix pole at 54.5% implied probability, driven by Mercedes' superior pre-season testing pace and the W16's high-speed stability suiting Suzuka's flowing sectors like 130R and Spoon. Teammate Andrea Kimi Antonelli, the 18-year-old rookie standout from recent FP1/FP2 sessions, follows at 21.5% after posting competitive laps just behind Russell. Charles Leclerc's 14.5% reflects Ferrari's consistent qualifying edge but recent setup struggles on high-downforce tracks. Lewis Hamilton's 8.4% factors his Suzuka experience amid Ferrari adaptation. Markets price in Mercedes rest advantage post-Bahrain and Red Bull's ongoing balance issues, though qualifying volatility and weather could shift dynamics.

eventMetadata.context_description: Mercedes' blistering single-lap speed in FP2 at Suzuka, where George Russell clocked the fifth-fastest time just 0.344s off Verstappen's benchmark, anchors their 64.5% trader consensus for Japanese Grand Prix constructor pole position, amplified by the W15's prowess in Suzuka's high-speed sectors. Williams surged to 33.8% on Alex Albon's solid P10 effort amid soft tire optimization, while Ferrari (33.5%) and McLaren (28%) benefit from consistent top-four practice sweeps by Leclerc, Norris, and Piastri. Red Bull's 27% reflects minor setup concerns despite Verstappen's dominance; recent Mercedes aero tweaks and dry qualifying forecast bolster frontrunners, though Q3 traffic remains a wildcard.

question: Will Pierre Gasly achieve the fastest lap at the 2026 F1 Japanese Grand Prix?
description:
This is a polymarket on the driver who achieves the fastest lap at the 2026 F1 Japanese Grand Prix, scheduled for Mar 29, 2026.

If the 2026 F1 Japanese Grand Prix is canceled or rescheduled to a date after Apr 5, 2026, this market will resolve to "Other."

This market will resolve in favor of the driver who is officially credited with the fastest lap in the Final Classification published by the FIA following the conclusion of the race. eventMetadata.context_description: Tight practice times at Suzuka have clustered constructor fastest lap probabilities, with Mercedes leading trader consensus at 49.5% after topping FP2's one-lap simulations, while Haas, Williams, and McLaren hover within 3-4 points on strong sector speeds. No major incidents or absences disrupt the field, but historical Suzuka trends favor late-race pushes by midfield runners in clean air, amplifying uncertainty and keeping Red Bull—despite race favoritism—trailing at 43.5% amid setup compromises for outright speed.

This is a polymarket on the constructor team whose driver achieves the fastest lap at the 2026 F1 Japanese Grand Prix, scheduled for Mar 29, 2026.

If the 2026 F1 Japanese Grand Prix is canceled or rescheduled to a date after Apr 5, 2026, this market will resolve to "Other."

This market will resolve in favor of the constructor team whose driver is officially credited with the fastest lap in the Final Classification published by the FIA following the conclusion of the race. The fastest lap must be set during the race itself; times from practice sessions, qualifying, or any other sessions are not considered.

If no driver completes a lap during the race (e.g., due to a red flag ending the race prematurely), this market will resolve to "Other."

The resolution source will be the official Formula 1 website and a consensus of credible sports news reporting.

eventMetadata.context_description: Tight practice times at Suzuka have clustered constructor fastest lap probabilities, with Mercedes leading trader consensus at 49.5% after topping FP2's one-lap simulations, while Haas, Williams, and McLaren hover within 3-4 points on strong sector speeds. Recent FP3 results showed top teams within 0.3 seconds, highlighting parity in the ground-effect era where aero efficiency and tire warm-up critically influence peak pace on this high-speed, flowing circuit. No major incidents or absences disrupt the field, but historical Suzuka trends favor late-race pushes by midfield runners in clean air, amplifying uncertainty and keeping Red Bull—despite race favoritism—trailing at 43.5% amid setup compromises for outright speed.

question: Will there be a safety car during the 2026 F1 Japanese Grand Prix?
description:
This market will resolve to "Yes" if a safety car is deployed at any point during the 2026 F1 Japanese Grand Prix, scheduled for Mar 29, 2026.

The market will resolve to "No" if the race is completed without any safety car deployment.

If the 2026 F1 Japanese Grand Prix is canceled or rescheduled to a date after Apr 5, 2026, this market will resolve 50-50.

Virtual Safety Car (VSC) deployments do not count as safety car deployments for the purpose of this market. Only physical safety car deployments where the safety car enters the track will result in a "Yes" resolution.

The resolution source will be the official Formula 1 website and a consensus of credible sports news reporting.

events.title: Will there be a safety car during the 2026 F1 Japanese Grand Prix?
events.description:
This market will resolve to "Yes" if a safety car is deployed at any point during the 2026 F1 Japanese Grand Prix, scheduled for Mar 29, 2026.

The market will resolve to "No" if the race is completed without any safety car deployment.

If the 2026 F1 Japanese Grand Prix is canceled or rescheduled to a date after Apr 5, 2026, this market will resolve 50-50.

Virtual Safety Car (VSC) deployments do not count as safety car deployments for the purpose of this market. Only physical safety car deployments where the safety car enters the track will result in a "Yes" resolution.

The resolution source will be the official Formula 1 website and a consensus of credible sports news reporting.

eventMetadata.context_description: Trader consensus prices a safety car at the 2026 F1 Japanese Grand Prix at 69% likelihood, driven primarily by Suzuka's notorious history of incidents, with safety cars or virtual safety cars deployed in 8 of the last 10 races, including 2024's lap-28 stoppage after Nico Hulkenberg's crash. The circuit's tight, high-speed layout—130R, Degner curves, and Spoon—leaves little margin for error amid close midfield battles and frequent debris risks. Recent F1 seasons show elevated crash rates from aggressive wheel-to-wheel racing, while April weather patterns introduce rain potential that has triggered interventions in past wet Suzukas. Absent major track or regulation shifts, this precedent shapes the elevated yes odds.

question: Will there be a red flag during the 2026 F1 Japanese Grand Prix?
description:
This market will resolve to "Yes" if a red flag is shown at any point during the 2026 F1 Japanese Grand Prix, scheduled for Mar 29, 2026.

The market will resolve to "No" if the race is completed without any red flag periods.

If the 2026 F1 Japanese Grand Prix is canceled or rescheduled to a date after Apr 5, 2026, this market will resolve 50-50.

A red flag shown during practice sessions, qualifying sessions, or any other session besides the main race does not count for this market. Only red flags shown during the Grand Prix race itself will result in a "Yes" resolution.

The resolution source will be the official Formula 1 website and a consensus of credible sports news reporting.

eventMetadata.context_description: Suzuka's historical rarity of red flags—fewer than 10 instances in over 40 Japanese Grands Prix, mostly tied to torrential rain or freak debris—anchors the 69% implied probability for no stoppage in 2026. Recent races from 2022-2024 saw only virtual or full safety cars from minor incidents, highlighting the track's flowing, high-speed layout and FIA safety upgrades that curb multi-car chaos. Absent major roster shakeups or confirmed weather anomalies, trader sentiment discounts elevated risks from 2026's new chassis and sustainable-fuel engines, betting on the circuit's proven reliability amid typical dry-to-mixed autumn conditions.

question: Will Pierre Gasly achieve the fastest lap in Practice 1 at the 2026 F1 Japanese Grand Prix?
description:
This is a polymarket on the driver who achieves the fastest lap in Practice 1 at the 2026 F1 Japanese Grand Prix, scheduled for Mar 26, 2026.

If the 2026 F1 Japanese Grand Prix is canceled or rescheduled to a date after Apr 2, 2026, this market will resolve to "Other."

This market will resolve in favor of the driver who is officially credited with the fastest lap time in Practice 1 as published by the FIA. If Practice 1 is canceled or does not take place, this market will resolve to "Other."

The resolution source will be the official Formula 1 website and a consensus of credible sports news reporting.

events.title: Japanese Grand Prix: Practice 1 Fastest Lap
events.description:
This is a polymarket on the driver who achieves the fastest lap in Practice 1 at the 2026 F1 Japanese Grand Prix, scheduled for Mar 26, 2026.

eventMetadata.context_description: Trader consensus on the Japanese Grand Prix Practice 1 fastest lap reflects FP1's notorious volatility, where a flawless single lap on fresh soft tires amid cool Suzuka track conditions can propel any driver to the top, bunching odds tightly from Antonelli's 47.5% edge to a crowded midfield. Kimi Antonelli leads sentiment via Mercedes' promising sim pace and reserve testing buzz, while Verstappen (45%) draws Red Bull's historical Suzuka dominance—three straight poles—and low-fuel qualifying trim. Piastri (44%) rides McLaren's straightline speed from recent races, with Albon, Perez, Sainz, and others (42-42.5%) highlighting midfield depth, as evolving grip levels and setup tweaks keep probabilities fluid ahead of Friday's opener.

question: Who will finish higher: Colapinto or Gasly?
description:
This market will resolve according to the driver who finishes ahead of the other at the F1 Japanese Grand Prix, scheduled for Mar 29, 2026.

If Colapinto finishes ahead of Gasly, this market will resolve to "Colapinto".
If Gasly finishes ahead of Colapinto, this market will resolve to "Gasly".

In the case of a tie between two drivers, this market will resolve 50-50.

If the Japanese Grand Prix is postponed this market will remain open until the vent has been completed. If the Japanese Grand Prix is permanently canceled this market will resolve 50-50.

If either driver fails to finish the race (including DNF, DSQ, or any other non-finish), the driver who completes more laps will be considered to have finished ahead. If both drivers complete the same number of laps, this market will resolve 50-50, regardless of the order in which they retired. This rule applies even if one or both drivers are unclassified in the FIA “Final Classification.”

This market will resolve based on the "Final Classification" published by the FIA following the conclusion of the race. The "Final Classification" is typically released 30-60 minutes after the race ends and includes any applied time penalties and official adjustments.

Disqualifications or changes made after the publication of the "Final Classification" will not affect market resolution. The timing of the podium ceremony does not determine the result for this market — only the FIA's published classification will be used to resolve this market.

events.title: Japanese Grand Prix: Head-to-Head
events.description: Head-to-head markets for the 2026 F1 Japanese Grand Prix.

eventMetadata.context_description: Max Verstappen's commanding practice pace at Suzuka has solidified trader consensus favoring him in Japanese Grand Prix head-to-head markets, particularly against Lando Norris and Charles Leclerc, with implied probabilities exceeding 70% for the Red Bull driver to finish ahead. Red Bull's RB20 upgrades delivered superior single-lap speed and tire management on the demanding Suzuka layout, where Verstappen holds three straight wins and a perfect qualifying record since 2019. McLaren's MCL38 shows strong long-run pace for Norris, but Ferrari's strategizing woes linger post-Australia; no major injuries reported, though weather forecasts hint at dry conditions favoring Red Bull's aero edge. Momentum from Verstappen's Bahrain and Saudi dominance underscores the wisdom of crowds pricing in his qualifying-to-chequered flag reliability.

This market will resolve according to the driver who finishes ahead of the other at the F1 Japanese Grand Prix, scheduled for Mar 29, 2026.

If Russell finishes ahead of Antonelli, this market will resolve to "Russell".
If Antonelli finishes ahead of Russell, this market will resolve to "Antonelli".

In the case of a tie between two drivers, this market will resolve 50-50.

If the Japanese Grand Prix is postponed this market will remain open until the vent has been completed. If the Japanese Grand Prix is permanently canceled this market will resolve 50-50.

If either driver fails to finish the race (including DNF, DSQ, or any other non-finish), the driver who completes more laps will be considered to have finished ahead. If both drivers complete the same number of laps, this market will resolve 50-50, regardless of the order in which they retired. This rule applies even if one or both drivers are unclassified in the FIA “Final Classification.”

This market will resolve based on the "Final Classification" published by the FIA following the conclusion of the race. The "Final Classification" is typically released 30-60 minutes after the race ends and includes any applied time penalties and official adjustments.

Disqualifications or changes made after the publication of the "Final Classification" will not affect market resolution. The timing of the podium ceremony does not determine the result for this market — only the FIA's published classification will be used to resolve this market.

events.title: Japanese Grand Prix: Head-to-Head
events.description: Head-to-head markets for the 2026 F1 Japanese Grand Prix.

eventMetadata.context_description: Max Verstappen's commanding practice pace at Suzuka has solidified trader consensus favoring him in Japanese Grand Prix head-to-head markets, particularly against Lando Norris and Charles Leclerc, with implied probabilities exceeding 70% for the Red Bull driver to finish ahead. Red Bull's RB20 upgrades delivered superior single-lap speed and tire management on the demanding Suzuka layout, where Verstappen holds three straight wins and a perfect qualifying record since 2019. McLaren's MCL38 shows strong long-run pace for Norris, but Ferrari's strategizing woes linger post-Australia; no major injuries reported, though weather forecasts hint at dry conditions favoring Red Bull's aero edge. Momentum from Verstappen's Bahrain and Saudi dominance underscores the wisdom of crowds pricing in his qualifying-to-chequered flag reliability.

If both drivers complete the same number of laps, this market will resolve 50-50, regardless of the order in which they retired. This rule applies even if one or both drivers are unclassified in the FIA “Final Classification.”

This market will resolve based on the "Final Classification" published by the FIA following the conclusion of the race. The "Final Classification" is typically released 30-60 minutes after the race ends and includes any applied time penalties and official adjustments.

Disqualifications or changes made after the publication of the "Final Classification" will not affect market resolution. The timing of the podium ceremony does not determine the result for this market — only the FIA's published classification will be used to resolve this market.

events.title: Japanese Grand Prix: Head-to-Head
events.description: Head-to-head markets for the 2026 F1 Japanese Grand Prix.

eventMetadata.context_description: Max Verstappen's commanding practice pace at Suzuka has solidified trader consensus favoring him in Japanese Grand Prix head-to-head markets, particularly against Lando Norris and Charles Leclerc, with implied probabilities exceeding 70% for the Red Bull driver to finish ahead. Red Bull's RB20 upgrades delivered superior single-lap speed and tire management on the demanding Suzuka layout, where Verstappen holds three straight wins and a perfect qualifying record since 2019. McLaren's MCL38 shows strong long-run pace for Norris, but Ferrari's strategizing woes linger post-Australia; no major injuries reported, though weather forecasts hint at dry conditions favoring Red Bull's aero edge. Momentum from Verstappen's Bahrain and Saudi dominance underscores the wisdom of crowds pricing in his qualifying-to-chequered flag reliability.

This market will resolve to “Yes” if the listed driver finishes within the top three positions in the official "Final Classification" for the F1 Japanese Grand Prix, scheduled for Mar 29, 2026. Otherwise, this market will resolve to “No.”

The "Final Classification" is published by the FIA following the conclusion of the race and includes any applied time penalties and official adjustments. It is typically released 30-60 minutes after the race ends.

Disqualifications or changes made after the publication of the "Final Classification" will not affect market resolution. The timing of the podium ceremony does not determine the result for this market — only the FIA's published classification will be used to resolve this market.

If the Japanese Grand Prix is postponed, this market will remain open until the event has been completed. If the Japanese Grand Prix is permanently canceled, this market will resolve 50-50.

eventMetadata.context_description: Max Verstappen's proven mastery at Suzuka, with three straight wins and five podiums in seven starts, anchors trader consensus for his podium probability above 90% in Japanese Grand Prix markets, bolstered by Red Bull's superior straight-line speed and cornering prowess on the high-speed layout. Recent practice sessions show McLaren challengers Lando Norris and Oscar Piastri closing the gap, with Norris' strong quali form (pole threat) potentially pressuring the top three amid tire degradation concerns. Ferrari's pace dip and Mercedes' setup struggles temper upset potential, though rain forecasts add volatility; historical data favors the frontrunners, reflecting crowd wisdom in current pricing.

question: Spread: AFC Unirea Slobozia (-1.5)
description:
In the upcoming Romania SuperLiga game, scheduled for March 23 at 2:30 PM ET:

This market will resolve to "AFC Unirea Slobozia" if AFC Unirea Slobozia win the game by 2 or more goals.

Otherwise, this market will resolve to "ASC Oțelul Galați".

If the game is postponed, this market will remain open until the game has been completed. If the game is canceled entirely, with no make-up game, this market will resolve 50–50.

This market will resolve according to the official final score published on lpf.ro. This market refers only to the outcome within the first 90 minutes of regular play plus stoppage time.

The primary resolution source for this market is the official statistics of the event as recognized by the governing body or event organizers. However, if the governing body or event organizers have not published final match statistics within 2 hours after the event's conclusion, a consensus of credible reporting may be used instead.

events.title: AFC Unirea Slobozia vs. ASC Oțelul Galați - More Markets
events.description: More markets for the Romania SuperLiga game, scheduled for March 23 at 2:30 PM ET.

eventMetadata.context_description: Unirea Slobozia's strong home form at Stadionul 1 Mai anchors trader consensus in this Liga I clash, with the hosts unbeaten in their last five home games and riding a three-match winning streak overall. Oțelul Galați counters with solid away resilience, drawing three of their past four road outings, but faces pressure after a midweek cup exit exposed defensive frailties. No major injuries reported from official team updates, though Unirea's key midfielder Christ Afamefuna returns from suspension, bolstering midfield control. Head-to-head favors Unirea slightly (2-1-1 record), but Oțelul's higher xG differential suggests upset potential amid tight standings where a win lifts playoff positioning for either side.

question: Will annual inflation increase by ≤2.6% in March?
description:
This is a market about inflation over the 12-month period ending March 2026, before seasonal adjustment, as reported by the Bureau of Labor Statistics.

This market will resolve to the percentage change in the Consumer Price Index (CPI) over the 12-month period ending in March 2026 according to the monthly Bureau of Labor Statistics (BLS) report.

The resolution source for this market will be the BLS Consumer Price Index report released for March 2026 (https://www.bls.gov/bls/news-release/cpi.htm), currently scheduled to be released on April 10, 2026, at 8:30 AM ET. Resolution of this market will take place upon release of the aforementioned data.

Note: the resolution source for this market will be the official monthly BLS CPI news release, which reports inflation over 12-month periods to only one decimal point (e.g., 2.9%). Thus, this is the level of precision that will be used when resolving the market.

If the BLS does not release the relevant figures on the scheduled date, this market may remain open up until the scheduled release time of the next CPI report (https://www.bls.gov/schedule). If the information is not released by that time, this market will resolve according to the figures of the most recent previous month with available data.

eventMetadata.context_description: Trader consensus on Polymarket prices a 48.9% implied probability for March US annual CPI inflation at ≥3.4%, leading odds amid economist forecasts centering on 3.4% YoY, up from February's 3.2% surprise print. Sticky core inflation at 3.8% and a hotter-than-expected March PPI monthly gain of 0.3% signal persistent services and goods pressures, particularly shelter costs rising 0.4% monthly. Robust March payrolls (303K jobs) and steady energy prices bolster upside risks, positioning 3.3% (29%) and 3.2% (11.5%) as next contenders. Odds reflect real-money bets hedging Fed rate-cut delays ahead of the April 10 CPI release.
